section.title: The Collection and Final Greetings
section.sectionRationale: The letter closes not with abstraction but with the concrete life of the church: a systematic collection for the poor saints in Jerusalem, Paul's travel plans and those of his coworkers, a bracing charge to be watchful and strong and to do everything in love, and warm final greetings. The resurrection hope of chapter fifteen now issues in generous giving, faithful labor, mutual love, and longing for the Lord's return, sealed with Maranatha, Come O Lord, and the grace of the Lord Jesus.
section.thesis: Gospel truth works itself out in ordinary faithfulness: giving systematically and cheerfully for the needy, laboring and traveling for the churches, standing firm and strong while doing everything in love, honoring fellow workers, and longing for the Lord's coming, so that the letter ends in grace, love, and the cry Maranatha, Come O Lord.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the collection for the saints; Paul's travel plans and his coworkers; the closing charge to be watchful, firm, strong, and loving; and the final greetings, the curse and the cry Maranatha, and the grace of Christ.

units.label: The collection for the saints
units.note: give systematically on the first day

verses.text: Now about the {{logeia|collection}} for the saints, you are to do as I directed the churches of Galatia:
On the first day of every week, each of you should set aside a portion of his income, saving it up, so that when I come no collections will be needed.
Then, on my arrival, I will send letters with those you recommend to carry your gift to Jerusalem.
And if it is advisable for me to go also, they can travel with me.

units.label: Travel plans and coworkers
units.note: an open door, and faithful helpers

verses.text: After I go through Macedonia, however, I will come to you; for I will be going through Macedonia.
Perhaps I will stay with you awhile, or even spend the winter, so that you can help me on my journey, wherever I go.
For I do not want to see you now only in passing; I hope to spend some time with you, if the Lord permits.
But I will stay in Ephesus until Pentecost,
because a great door for effective work has opened to me, even though many oppose me.
If Timothy comes, see to it that he has nothing to fear while he is with you, for he is doing the work of the Lord, just as I am.
No one, then, should treat him with contempt. Send him on his way in peace so that he can return to me, for I am expecting him along with the brothers.
Now about our brother Apollos: I strongly urged him to go to you with the brothers. He was not at all inclined to go now, but he will go when he has the opportunity.

units.label: Be watchful, firm, strong, and loving

verses.text: Be on the {{gregoreo|alert}}. Stand firm in the faith. Be men of courage. Be strong.
Do everything in {{agape|love}}.
You know that Stephanas and his household were the first converts in Achaia, and they have devoted themselves to the service of the saints. Now I urge you, brothers,
to submit to such as these, and to every fellow worker and laborer.
I am glad that Stephanas, Fortunatus, and Achaicus have arrived, because they have supplied what was lacking from you.
For they refreshed my spirit and yours as well. Show your appreciation, therefore, to such men.

units.label: Final greetings and Maranatha
units.note: Come, O Lord, and grace in Christ

verses.text: The churches in the province of Asia send you greetings. Aquila and Prisca greet you warmly in the Lord, and so does the church that meets at their house.
All the brothers here send you greetings. Greet one another with a holy kiss.
This greeting is in my own hand, Paul.
If anyone does not love the Lord, let him be under a curse. Come, O Lord!
The {{charis|grace}} of the Lord Jesus be with you.
My love be with all of you in Christ Jesus. Amen.

logeia.gloss: A collection, a gathering of money. Paul uses the word for the relief fund he was organizing among the Gentile churches for the poor believers in Jerusalem. It was not a spontaneous appeal but a planned, regular, church-wide act of love that bound the young churches to the mother church in shared grace.

gregoreo.gloss: To be awake, to stay alert, to keep watch. The first of four sharp commands that close the body of the letter. It calls the church to spiritual wakefulness against error and temptation, a watchfulness that fits a people awaiting the Lord's return.

agape.gloss: Love, the self-giving love supremely displayed in Christ and celebrated in chapter thirteen. Here it caps and governs every other command: do everything in love. Courage and strength and firmness are all to be exercised in love, and Paul ends the letter with his own love to the church in Christ Jesus.

charis.gloss: Grace, God's unmerited favor in Christ. Paul opened the letter with grace and now closes it the same way: the grace of the Lord Jesus be with you. Everything in between, correction, teaching, and hope, is bracketed by grace, the free favor of God that both saves and sustains His people.

basic.context: Paul brings the letter to a close with practical matters. He gives instructions for a collection to help the poor Christians in Jerusalem: set money aside every week, planned and regular, so it is ready when he comes. He shares his travel plans, commends Timothy and mentions Apollos, and then fires off a series of short commands: be watchful, stand firm, be courageous, be strong, and do everything in love. He honors faithful workers, sends greetings, and ends with a longing cry for the Lord to come and a blessing of grace.
basic.hermeneutics: These closing verses show doctrine landing in ordinary church life. The teaching of the whole letter, and especially the resurrection hope, now takes the shape of generous giving, faithful labor, watchful courage, and mutual love. Paul's personal plans and greetings are not mere filler; they reveal a network of real churches and workers bound together in the gospel, and they model how truth becomes practice.
basic.originalLanguages: The collection is a logeia, a planned, regular gathering of gifts for the needy. The closing charge opens with gregoreo, be watchful, a call to spiritual wakefulness. Every command is then governed by agape, love: do everything in love. And the letter ends as it began, with charis, grace, the grace of the Lord Jesus be with you.
basic.typology: The collection for Jerusalem echoes the sharing of Israel's tribes and points to the one new people of God, Jew and Gentile, united in Christ and caring for one another. The first day of the week, the day of resurrection, becomes the rhythm of Christian giving and gathering, a weekly witness to the risen Lord.
basic.christ: Christ is the center even of these closing lines. The giving flows from His grace, the labor is the work of the Lord, the love is His love, and the great longing is for His return, Maranatha, Come O Lord. The letter ends where it must, in the grace of the Lord Jesus and love in Christ Jesus.
basic.god: God is the giver of the open door for effective work, the Lord who permits or redirects our plans, and the source of the grace that frames the whole letter. He is building one people from many nations, and He calls them to love, to give, to stand firm, and to wait eagerly for His Son.
basic.crossReferences: Second Corinthians 8 and 9 unfold the same Jerusalem collection and the theology of cheerful giving. Acts 20 records Paul's travels through Macedonia and his companions. Revelation 22 echoes the cry Come, Lord Jesus. Romans 15 explains the offering for the poor saints in Jerusalem.
basic.oneStory: The letter ends by weaving the young Corinthian church into the worldwide people of God: giving to distant believers, hosting traveling workers, greeting sister churches, and crying out together for the Lord to come. The grand truths of resurrection and gospel come down to a weekly offering, a firm stand, a loving deed, and a longing heart, all held in the grace of Christ.

context.literary: Chapter sixteen is the letter's epilogue, moving through discrete practical items: the collection, verses 1 to 4; travel plans and coworkers, verses 5 to 12; a staccato charge and commendation, verses 13 to 18; and final greetings with a solemn benediction, verses 19 to 24. The four terse imperatives of verse 13, crowned by verse 14, form a memorable summary of the whole letter's concerns.
context.historical: The collection for the Jerusalem church was a major project of Paul's ministry, uniting Gentile congregations in tangible care for the poor mother church. Corinth sat on trade routes between Ephesus, where Paul then was, and Macedonia and Jerusalem. Aquila and Prisca, the household churches, and the named visitors reflect the real, mobile, interconnected world of the early churches.
context.canonical: The chapter connects to 2 Corinthians 8 to 9 and Romans 15, which develop the same relief offering and its meaning. The closing Maranatha preserves an Aramaic prayer from the earliest Christian worship, echoed in Revelation 22:20, showing how central the hope of Christ's return was from the very beginning.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To organize the relief collection wisely, to arrange his own and his coworkers' visits, and to leave the church with a compact charge to watchfulness, steadfastness, courage, strength, and love, all sealed with the hope of Christ's coming and the grace of the Lord Jesus.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul's travel plans and greetings are description, telling us what was happening, not commands for us. The prescriptive core is the instruction to give regularly and the charge of verses 13 to 14: be watchful, stand firm, be strong, do everything in love. These abiding commands apply to every church.

debates.question: Does giving on the first day of every week require a fixed rule for Christian giving today?

views.label: A wise pattern, not a binding law
views.case: Paul gives practical instruction so the gift is ready and orderly when he comes; it models regular, planned, proportionate giving tied to the resurrection day, but it is not framed as a new law with a fixed percentage.

views.label: An enduring principle of systematic giving
views.case: The weekly, proportionate, everyone-participating pattern reflects abiding wisdom for the church, teaching believers to give deliberately and consistently rather than by impulse.

debates.bearing: Either way the passage commends planned, regular, cheerful, proportionate giving as a normal part of church life, without turning it into legalism; the fuller theology of glad generosity appears in 2 Corinthians 8 to 9.

study.originalLanguages: The closing chapter carries a few telling words. The relief fund is a logeia, a planned and regular collection for the poor saints, not a spontaneous appeal. The final charge opens with gregoreo, be watchful, a summons to spiritual wakefulness in a people awaiting their Lord. Over all the commands stands agape, love: do everything in love, so that courage and firmness are never harsh. And the whole letter is bracketed by charis, grace, opening and now closing with the grace of the Lord Jesus, while the Aramaic cry Maranatha, Come O Lord, voices the church's deepest longing.

typology.fulfillment: Embodies the one new people of God, Jew and Gentile joined in Christ, bearing one another's burdens as the tribes of Israel once shared and as the body of Christ now cares for its members.

typology.type: The first day of the week
typology.fulfillment: The day of Christ's resurrection becomes the rhythm of Christian gathering and giving, a weekly witness to the risen Lord and a foretaste of the age to come.

study.christ: Though the chapter is practical, Christ is everywhere in it. The giving is an overflow of His grace, the labor is the work of the Lord, and the love that must govern everything is His own love poured into His people. The letter's final longing is fixed on Him: Maranatha, Come O Lord, the church's cry for the return of her risen King. And Paul signs off in the only place a Christian letter can rest, the grace of the Lord Jesus and love in Christ Jesus.
study.god: God opens doors for effective work even amid opposition, governs the plans of His servants, if the Lord permits, and knits His scattered churches into one caring family. He is the ultimate giver behind every gift for the saints, and the fountain of the grace that frames the whole letter. The God who raised Christ now calls His people to watchful, loving, generous lives as they await His Son.

crossReferences.note: The same Jerusalem collection, grounded in the grace of the Lord Jesus who became poor to make us rich.
God loves a cheerful giver, the heart behind the systematic giving urged here.
Paul carries the offering of the Gentile churches to the poor saints in Jerusalem, the project of verses 1 to 4.
Come, Lord Jesus, echoing the Maranatha of verse 22 and the church's longing for His return.
Paul's journey through Macedonia with his companions, matching the travel plans of verses 5 to 6.

study.oneStory: The letter closes by folding the Corinthian church into the worldwide people of God: giving to distant believers, welcoming traveling workers, exchanging greetings with sister churches, and crying out together, Maranatha, Come O Lord. The towering truths of resurrection and gospel come to earth as a weekly offering, a firm stand, a courageous and loving deed, and a longing heart, all held within the grace of the Lord Jesus that opened and now seals the letter.

quiz.q: What does Paul instruct about the collection for the saints?

options.text: Each is to set aside money on the first day of every week, so the gift is ready when he comes.
options.note: Regular, planned, proportionate giving so no last-minute collections are needed.

options.text: A single large gift should be demanded from the wealthy only.
options.note: Paul asks each one to give, weekly and proportionately, not only the wealthy.

options.text: Give generously by impulse whenever moved, with no set pattern.
options.note: Generosity is right, but Paul urges deliberate, weekly setting aside, not only impulse.

options.text: The church should not give to believers elsewhere.
options.note: The whole point is a gift for the poor saints in Jerusalem.

quiz.q: What are the four commands in verse 13?

options.text: Be on the alert, stand firm in the faith, be courageous, be strong.
options.note: A compact charge to watchfulness, steadfastness, courage, and strength.

options.text: Give, pray, fast, and tithe.
options.note: Good practices, but not the four commands of this verse.

options.text: Be silent, be still, be hidden, be safe.
options.note: The commands call for active watchfulness and courage, not withdrawal.

options.text: Stand firm, be strong, be humble, and be quiet.
options.note: Stand firm and be strong are right, but the verse calls for watchfulness and courage, not humility and quiet.

quiz.q: What governs all the commands, according to verse 14?

options.text: Love: do everything in love.
options.note: Courage and strength are all to be exercised in love.

options.text: Reverence and the fear of the Lord.
options.note: Godly reverence matters, but Paul names love as what must govern everything.

options.text: Personal ambition.
options.note: The opposite: everything is to be done in love, not ambition.

options.text: Strict rule-keeping.
options.note: Not rule-keeping but love is to shape everything.

quiz.q: What does the cry Maranatha express?

options.text: The longing prayer of the church, Come, O Lord, for Christ's return.
options.note: An early Aramaic prayer voicing the hope of the Lord's coming.

options.text: A pronouncement of judgment on those who do not love the Lord.
options.note: That is the preceding clause, let him be under a curse; Maranatha itself is the prayer, Come, O Lord.

options.text: A closing word about the collection for the saints.
options.note: The collection was earlier in the chapter; Maranatha is a prayer for the Lord's coming.

options.text: A greeting to the churches of Asia.
options.note: The greetings are separate; Maranatha is a cry for Christ to come.

quiz.q: How does Paul end the letter?

options.text: With the grace of the Lord Jesus and his love in Christ Jesus.
options.note: The letter closes in grace and love, framing everything in Christ.

options.text: With a final list of rules to obey.
options.note: He closes with grace and love, not a rulebook.

options.text: With a warning that all is hopeless.
options.note: He ends in grace, love, and the hope of Christ's coming.

options.text: With greetings and a holy kiss, but no final blessing.
options.note: He does send greetings, but the letter closes with the grace of the Lord Jesus and his love in Christ.

questions: Paul asks each believer to set aside a gift regularly on the first day of the week. What would planned, cheerful, proportionate giving look like in your own life?
An open door for effective work stood before Paul even though many opposed him. Where might God be opening a door for you in the face of opposition?
Verse 13 calls you to be watchful, firm, courageous, and strong. Which of these do you most need to grow in right now, and how?
Everything is to be done in love. Where are you tempted to be right, or strong, or firm without love, and how would love reshape it?
The church cried Maranatha, Come O Lord. How alive is your longing for Christ's return, and how might it change the way you live this week?

translationNotes.note: The first day of every week is Sunday, the day of Christ's resurrection, which was becoming the Christian day of gathering; the instruction ties regular giving to that weekly rhythm.
Prisca is the same person called Priscilla in Acts; Paul uses the more formal form of her name alongside her husband Aquila.
Maranatha is an Aramaic expression preserved from the earliest church, rendered here Come, O Lord; it voices the church's prayer for Christ's return and is echoed in Revelation 22:20.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 16:1-24. Any em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas to comply with the no-dash style rule; wording is unchanged.
